Infra & Chips.content: **BREAKING: [Futures Rise After Market Hits Highs](https://www.investors.com/market-trend/stock-market-today/dow-jones-futures-sp-500-nasdaq-micron-1-trillion/)** --- * [Research](https://www.investors.com/category/research/) # Nvidia Wavers After Weekly Loss Follows Earnings Beat; Is Nvidia A Buy Now? **Nvidia** ([NVDA](https://research.investors.com/quote.aspx?symbol=NVDA)) stock on Tuesday wavered after a 4% loss last week despite the chipmaker delivering an earnings beat for its first quarter. Recently, the stock hit an all-time high while reclaiming its market cap of $5 trillion amid a seven-day winning streak. So, is Nvidia stock a [buy or sell?](https://www.investors.com/research/nvidia-stock-nvda-buy-or-sell-what-investors-need-to-know-jensen-huang/) ↑ X [How AI Agents Are Changing Who Gets Hired At A $1 Billion Company](https://www.investors.com/ibd-videos/videos/how-ai-agents-are-changing-who-gets-hired-at-a-1-billion-company) [See All Videos](https://www.investors.com/ibd-videos) NOW PLAYING How AI Agents Are Changing Who Gets Hired At A $1 Billion Company The artificial intelligence chipmaker last week [posted earnings](https://www.investors.com/news/technology/nvidia-stock-nvda-fiscal-q1-2027-earnings/) of $1.87 a share, topping Wall Street's consensus estimate of $1.75 per share. Sales of $81.6 billion surpassed expectations of $78.9 billion. Data center revenue grew 92% annually as demand had gone "parabolic," Nvidia Chief Executive Jensen Huang said. Nvidia [boosted its quarterly dividend](https://www.investors.com/news/technology/nvidia-stock-price-target-hikes-q1-earnings-report/) to 25 cents per share from 1 cent per share. Also, management increased its stock repurchase program to $118.5 billion. On May 15, Nvidia stock was hit after President Donald Trump's remarks following his trip to Beijing. He said [China has yet to buy](https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/iran-us-china-news-2026/card/china-hasn-t-bought-nvidia-s-h200-chips-trump-says-gpzR19U84duc9kNmz7i1) Nvidia's H200 AI chip for the local market, even though the U.S. had cleared the way for such purchases. During the mid-May meeting between Trump and China's President Xi Jinping in Beijing, Nvidia shares climbed. Xi reportedly said "China will only open its door wider" to U.S. businesses. Also, China's state-backed news outlet [Xinhua](https://english.news.cn/20260514/169d2354fea2419d99be12457a05883c/c.html) reported that Xi and Trump agreed on a deeper collaboration. However, Nvidia shares fell after the meeting, amid wider weakness among chip stocks. The meeting ended with no major trade announcements, apart from 10 Chinese companies receiving clearance for buying Nvidia's H200 chips, [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/business/retail-consumer/us-clears-h200-chip-sales-10-china-firms-nvidia-ceo-looks-breakthrough-2026-05-14/) reported. Huang, who went with other Big Tech CEOs with Trump to Beijing, said China represents a $50 billion opportunity for his company. Its H200 AI processor for the Chinese market is designed to meet U.S. export restrictions and was approved for sales to 10 Chinese companies. ## Nvidia Stock: Price Performance In terms of its 12-month price performance, Nvidia has outperformed 78% of [all other stocks](https://www.investors.com/how-to-invest/investors-corner/relative-strength-rating-stock-chart-analysis-helps-pick-outstanding-growth-stocks/) in Investor's Business Daily's database. Funds own 41% of the company's outstanding shares, according to [IBD MarketSurge](https://get.investors.com/marketsurge/?artProdLink=MarketSurge) research. But the stock's [Accumulation/Distribution Rating of B](https://www.investors.com/how-to-invest/investors-corner/stocks-funds-are-buying/) indicates strong recent fund purchases. Infra & Chips.title: SoftBank joins sovereign AI race - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.titleZh: SoftBank joins sovereign AI race - Light Reading
Infra & Chips.content: * [AI & Machine Learning](/digital-transformation/ai-machine-learning) * [Data Centers](/it-infrastructure/data-centers) * [News](/latest-news) # SoftBank joins sovereign AI race Japanese telco SoftBank enters the sovereign AI race with planned AI data center cloud in what is the latest move into enterprise AI by Asian telcos. [Robert Clark,](/author/robert-clark)Contributing Editor,Light Reading May 26, 2026 2 Min Read (Source: SOPA Images Limited/Alamy Stock Photo) SoftBank is set to enter the sovereign AI business, unveiling new GPU cloud infrastructure targeted to deploy in October. It[says](https://www.softbank.jp/en/corp/news/press/sbkk/2026/20260525_01/) its new AI data center cloud, built on NVIDIA GB200 and other advanced chips, will enable customers to carry out AI workloads while ensuring secure data operations within Japan. The new hardware combines with SoftBank's Infrinia AI Cloud OS, which supports Kubernetes-as-a-service (KaaS)\*3 and inference-as-a-service (Inf-aaS) for LLMs. CEO Junichi Miyakawa said the new cloud platform would "provide integrated computing infrastructure and software that can be securely used within Japan as a neocloud provider." He said that as AI becomes increasingly important as a source of competitiveness, customers need additional capabilities such as compute power and the operational software to support it. SoftBank Corp, the telecom arm of the SoftBank Group, says the next goal for its AI infrastructure will be to integrate the GPU cloud with AI-RAN while building out its distributed and sovereign AI capability. The closely related sovereign AI and GPU cloud markets are still nascent services for Asian telcos. They're not competing directly with hyperscalers but are serving their own customers who seek a trusted local provider. ##### Military-specific AI SoftBank's domestic rival NTT Data has built out a sovereign AI cloud platform targeted at enterprise as well as GPU-based infrastructure offerings. It has just [introduced enterprise AI factories](https://www.nttdata.com/global/en/news/press-release/2026/march/031200) to provide domain-specific AI solutions and also offers Tsuzumi, a language and enterprise-focused LLM. South Korea's SK Telecom has been[running its GPU-as-a-service](https://www.sktelecom.com/en/press/press_detail.do?idx=1629) since January 2025. At MWC in March it[unveiled a sovereign AI package](https://www.sktelecom.com/en/press/press_detail.do?idx=1659) that integrates its AI data center, sovereign AI model, and agentic and other AI services. It has[just partnered with the Defense Ministry](https://en.yna.co.kr/view/AEN20260514006500320) to develop and verify military-specific AI capabilities based on SKT's AI foundation model. The other regional telco active in sovereign AI is Singtel. It[debuted RE:AI](https://www.re-ai.ai/about-us/newsroom/singtel-launches-ai-cloud-service), its AI cloud and sovereign AI offering, in October 2024 with the stated aim of making AI capabilities more widely available. In its latest move[RE:AI has teamed up with France's Mistral AI](https://www.singtel.com/about-us/media-centre/news-releases/singtel-mistral-ai) to develop AI use cases in verticals including defense, financial services and healthcare. Singtel CEO Yuen Kuan Moon[said last week](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/21/singtel-earnings-ceo-ai-services-data-centers-.html) the company had allocated 1.2 billion Singapore dollars (US$940 million) for GPU as a service and sovereign AI. Read more about: [Asia](/keyword/asia) ## About the Author [Robert Clark](/author/robert-clark) Contributing Editor, Light Reading Robert Clark is an independent technology editor and researcher based in Hong Kong. [Search Federal Contracts](https://govtribe.com/) By Cale Stephens, Vice President, Crystal Group By Cale Stephens | ## Deploying AI at the tactical edge isn't a software problem. It's a full-stack engineering challenge that commercial architectures weren't built to solve, writes Cale Stephens, vice president at Crystal Group. * [Commentary](/topic/commentary/?oref=wt-article-topics) * [artificial intelligence](/topic/artificial-intelligence/?oref=wt-article-topics) Artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming military operations and the conversation is shifting. The defense community is no longer asking whether AI can deliver operational value. The focus now is how to deploy AI reliably in the environments where missions occur: at the tactical edge. That shift changes everything. Most commercial AI architectures were designed around centralized cloud infrastructure, abundant power, stable connectivity, and controlled operating conditions. Tactical environments offer none of those conveniences. Forward-deployed systems must operate in extreme temperatures, high-vibration platforms, contested electromagnetic environments, and disconnected or degraded networks, all while delivering real-time decision support to operators who cannot tolerate latency or system failure. As AI adoption accelerates across intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (ISR), autonomy, sensor fusion, electronic warfare, and predictive maintenance applications, engineering teams are confronting a difficult reality: advanced AI models are only as effective as the infrastructure supporting them. Compute performance alone is no longer enough. Running AI workloads at the edge requires a fundamentally different approach to system design. High-density GPUs and accelerators generate significant thermal loads, demand reliable high output power, and often exceed the size, weight, and power constraints of tactical platforms. Systems originally engineered for commercial data centers frequently struggle when exposed to shock, airborne vibration, dust intrusion, or intermittent connectivity. The challenge is not simply ruggedizing hardware after the fact. It is designing infrastructure from the outset to sustain AI operations in austere environments. That distinction matters because the tactical edge introduces operational requirements that cloud-native architectures were never intended to solve. Data must often be processed locally ...

portfolio for AI server and data center power systems at PCIM Europe 2026, which will be held from June 9 to 11, 2026, in Nuremberg, Germany. This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: <https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260526332549/en/> Magnachip at PCIM Europe 2026 At Hall 6, Booth 337, Magnachip will exhibit its MV MOSFET solutions for server and data center power supply units (PSUs), high-performance computing (HPC), and industrial power systems. As AI workloads continue to expand and data center power consumption rises, power efficiency, power density, and thermal management have become increasingly important in server and data center power system design. According to market research firm Omdia, the global power discrete semiconductor market for Computing and Data Storage applications is expected to grow from approximately $2.3 billion in 2025 to $3.0 billion in 2029, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 7.4%. portfolio for AI server and data center power systems at PCIM Europe 2026, which will be held from June 9 to 11, 2026, in Nuremberg, Germany.
